era_context: The early 11th century continued the patterns of the previous year: the Fatimid Caliphate under regency, the consolidation of feudal Europe, the Song Dynasty's cultural and economic flourishing in China, and the Byzantine Empire's military campaigns in the Balkans and Anatolia. This was a period of significant religious reform movements in Western Europe and the development of feudal hierarchies.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe and Scandinavia (limited contemporary sources)

events.title: Council of Puy-en-Velay (Peace of God movement)
events.description: A major ecclesiastical council was held at Puy-en-Velay in southern France, continuing the Peace of God movement that had begun in the 990s CE. The council sought to limit feudal violence and protect the Church and non-combatants through religious sanctions and oaths. Bishops and nobles gathered to enforce peace through excommunication and interdict. This movement represented an early attempt by the Church to regulate warfare and protect civilians, and it had significant influence on the development of chivalric codes and the later Crusading movement.
key_figures: Regional nobility and bishops of Aquitaine

sources.name: Later chronicles and ecclesiastical records (e.g., Ademar of Chabannes)
Historiographic consensus on the Peace of God movement

events.certainty_note: The Peace of God movement is well-attested, but the specific dating of individual councils is sometimes uncertain. 1005 CE is a traditional date for a major council at Puy-en-Velay, but some sources place it in 1010 CE or give a range of 1000–1020 CE.

events.title: Zhenzong's reign continues; Song Dynasty cultural flourishing
events.description: The Song Dynasty under Emperor Zhenzong (r. 997–1022 CE) continued its period of cultural, technological, and economic flourishing. In 1005 CE, the Song court was engaged in diplomatic negotiations with the Khitan Liao Dynasty to the north, resulting in the Shanyuan Treaty (1005 CE). This treaty established a peace that would last for over a century and allowed the Song to focus on internal development. The period saw advances in printing, ceramics, landscape painting, and Neo-Confucian philosophy.
events.certainty_note: The Shanyuan Treaty of 1005 CE is well-documented in Chinese historical records.

events.title: Shanyuan Treaty between Song Dynasty and Khitan Liao
events.description: The Shanyuan Treaty was signed in 1005 CE between the Song Dynasty and the Khitan Liao Dynasty, establishing a peace agreement after decades of military conflict. Under the treaty, the Song agreed to pay annual tribute to the Liao in silver and silk, and the Liao recognized Song sovereignty over southern territories. This treaty was a diplomatic victory for the Song, as it allowed them to redirect military resources and focus on economic development. The treaty remained in effect for over a century and became a model for later diplomatic arrangements in East Asia.
events.certainty_note: The treaty is well-documented in Chinese historical records with specific dates and terms.

events.title: Byzantine military campaigns in the Balkans
events.description: Emperor Basil II of the Byzantine Empire continued his military campaigns in the Balkans, consolidating Byzantine control over the region. In 1005 CE, Byzantine forces were engaged in campaigns against the Bulgarian Empire and other regional powers. Basil II's campaigns would culminate in the conquest of Bulgaria by 1018 CE, earning him the epithet 'Bulgar-slayer' (Bulgaroktonos). These campaigns represented the height of Byzantine military power and territorial expansion.

sources.name: Byzantine chronicles (e.g., Constantine VII Porphyrogennetus, De Administrando Imperio)
Historiographic consensus on Basil II's campaigns

events.certainty_note: Basil II's Balkan campaigns are well-attested, but the specific events of 1005 CE are less precisely documented than his later campaigns. The year is assigned based on the general chronology of his military activities.

disconfirming_evidence: The Council of Puy-en-Velay's date is disputed; some sources place it in 1010 CE or give a broader range. The Peace of God movement is well-attested as a phenomenon, but individual councils' dates are sometimes uncertain. The Shanyuan Treaty is sometimes dated to late 1004 CE or early 1005 CE depending on the calendar system used (lunar vs. proleptic Gregorian).
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1005 CE is moderate, with strong records for China and the Byzantine Empire, moderate records for Western Europe, and sparse records for other regions. Chinese sources are contemporary and reliable. Byzantine sources are often later compilations. Western European sources are fragmentary and often later compilations. The Peace of God movement is attested in later ecclesiastical records, which may reflect later idealization of the movement's scope and impact.

graph_edges.note: The Shanyuan Treaty enabled the Song Dynasty's continued cultural and economic flourishing
The Peace of God movement influenced the development of chivalric codes and later contributed to the ideological framework of the Crusades
Basil II's 1005 CE campaigns in the Balkans were part of the broader campaign that culminated in the conquest of Bulgaria by 1018 CE
